questions.question: Explain the SARSA algorithm and derive its update rule. How does it differ from Q-learning in terms of on-policy vs off-policy learning?
keyPoints: Definition of SARSA as on-policy TD control
Full derivation of update rule Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γQ(s',a') - Q(s,a)]
Explanation of each parameter (α, γ)
On-policy nature: same policy selects current and next action
Q-learning uses max, SARSA uses sampled action
Convergence implications of each approach

questions.question: Compare and contrast SARSA with Q-learning. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: SARSA is on-policy, Q-learning is off-policy
SARSA considers exploration in updates, Q-learning does not
SARSA converges to optimal ε-greedy policy, Q-learning to optimal greedy
Use SARSA when exploration is costly/risky
Use Q-learning when exploration is safe and you want optimal policy
Real-world examples (robotics vs game playing)

questions.question: What is Expected SARSA? How does it address the limitations of standard SARSA?
keyPoints: Expected SARSA uses expectation over all next actions
Update: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γΣₐ π(a'|s')Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]
Reduces variance compared to sampling single action
More stable learning process
Can be used on-policy or off-policy

questions.question: Why is SARSA considered more conservative than Q-learning? Give a practical example.
keyPoints: SARSA learns value of behavior policy including exploration
Accounts for possibility of exploration causing poor outcomes
Example: Robot near obstacles - SARSA learns safer path
Q-learning may learn optimal but risky path
SARSA preferred when mistakes have real consequences
